title: Interconnected Biogeochemical Cycles
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive network diagram showing how the carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles are linked through shared ecological processes

learningObjectives: Identify linkages between the carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles
Explain how disrupting one biogeochemical cycle affects others through shared processes
Predict cascading effects of human activities on interconnected biogeochemical cycles
prerequisites: Basic understanding of individual biogeochemical cycles
Familiarity with decomposition, photosynthesis, and nutrient runoff

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Systems thinking through interactive network exploration
keyQuestions: How are the four major biogeochemical cycles connected?
What shared processes link multiple cycles together?
How does disrupting one cycle cascade to affect others?
Why are environmental problems rarely confined to a single biogeochemical cycle?
What role do soil processes play in connecting all four cycles?
commonMisconceptions: Students often study each biogeochemical cycle in isolation and fail to recognize the deep connections between them
Many students think nutrient cycles are simple closed loops rather than interconnected networks with multiple pathways and feedback mechanisms
Students may not realize that disrupting one cycle can have unexpected consequences in seemingly unrelated cycles
assessmentOpportunities: Students create a connections matrix showing which cycles share which processes
Students write a cascade analysis tracing how one environmental problem propagates through multiple cycles
Students predict effects of specific human activities on interconnected cycles and justify their reasoning
